speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Not when the first time, but a little later on...

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: But most of the time in the early days we were 10, 12, 14, 15 hours a week.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come so long?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Seems like a pretty long day. Well, I made all the hand molded bricks. I had help. Usually you'd make big hand mold bricks for the railroad. That was all hand work then. We had molds. We'd stop the clay in and pound it down. And then sand the mold and close all the mold. And then it had to dry for several days. And it was quite a process. But they had such poor accommodations, poor buildings. It was a mess. And we had a big round kiln where we'd take the top off the bricks first. We held up to 60 ,000 bricks, some of them, if they weren't their bricks. And we'd put a plank in, climb about 30 degrees wheel bars and put 100 bricks on the wheel bar. And first we only had iron wheel bars. And then we finally got rubber tired. And I tell you when you got a whole dumb handle you just had to put all the weight on them. Go like fighting and pick up the handle and then you get down to the bottom. I tell you now, that wasn't a job. That was just, oh, I weighed probably 135 pounds then. But, you know, I stuck with it because we needed money. And I tell you, jobs are scarce.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they paying at that time?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Well, later on they paid up probably. During the war they paid four and a half a day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Two, World War II?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: And, let's see, somewhere around there. World War I, I wasn't there much, only a year or two then. It wasn't that much then. World War II, men went to Dr. Woods where they made big money, went hard to get help. They promised us they'd give us four and a half a day. And I don't remember, it was a ten hour day I think. Phone rings.

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Annie?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Uh huh. Oh, and they said they'd remember us. They sure did. Soon the war is over, they cut us down to four dollars.

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: This was the plan. They wanted money for everything. Oh, this is Annie, you know, I don't know too much about that. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But, uh, the conditions, have you got to go someplace?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: I've got to go and get the mail now. Or the bank. Well, that's about all I can tell you about. You can tell me now. I want to ask you about what they had. Well, they're in the middle of the street with the horses. And, uh, they had two or three sawmills around here. They put the sawdust in on Main Street here. And the logs, you can dig down, there'd be one log on top of another.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: You suppose I could find it on the top of the street?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: It was still in pretty good shape.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: It would have to give you any idea?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Been under water, see. And it was almost impossible to go through Main Street. Well, you see, horses stuck here and, you know. And there's more timber all around. And there were, well, there were some brick buildings. But a lot of them were just wooden buildings with a false front on them. And, uh, they had two blacksmith shops because one of them couldn't handle all the business. And they, there were no water systems here. They just, almost between every building there was a well, dug well, all over the ground. And the old houses here, most of them had picket fences around them. The cows were mine every year. Painting with, uh, the cows were mine paint, which was cheap. And there was, there was a place that had a barn, had a cow. And the kids would come to school, they had to come on horseback, or else they could have bought a horse. The rest of them walked, they had. So, things had changed an awful lot. When we first started here, it was a little before my time, they had Swedish school here. And no wooden building up there. But that was a little before my time. And now, most of the kids, they could talk Swedish. Just as good as English. But, after a while they didn't talk Swedish around here, mixed with the Americans and the Germans and Norwegians and what have you. The most of them, after a few years, couldn't talk English. I mean Swedish.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you remember people talking about Marshall Hayes during this?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Yes, yes. I went to school with some of his, uh, distant relatives. And I think, whether it was Johnny and Alfred Hayes, I think, if I'm not mistaken, I think John Hayes is still living down in Lewiston someplace. And I remember Granny Hayes had a square right over the cross from the bank up there. And she had cows and a picket fence around and a barn.

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Alfred, she was kind of a nice lady.

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Yeah, she was just a little bit hot -tempered, but she was good. She kept bigger and further. And Henry Hayes, he lived up this way then. But that was actually before I was here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think his reputation was deserved, Marshall Hayes?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Well, he had a lot of enemies. There wasn't many people like him, from what I hear. But they had a boarding house up across the other side of the street. And they had a sign down here at the corner of the hotel that says he was going to go up and stop the disturbance. And they told him to get any closer to shoot. And I met the guy here about 15, 20 years ago. His name was Payne Clyde. He was the one that shot Hayes. And he was down here. He had a big car. I think he spent 10 or 15 years in jail. But he said, that's the worst thing I ever go through. He says, every night I wake up and I can see that in front of me. He says, I tell you, it's a punishment, he said. You know, he can't get that out of his mind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does he himself shoot Hayes?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: He shot Hayes. He shot him from quite a ways off. He told him if he comes in further, he'll shoot. And he did. He must have been a pretty good shot. He fired one to get the sign down the corner of the hotel. That's hearsay now. Yeah, that's what I've heard too. Oh, he was good, I guess. He was tough. But then, they had to be tough. At one time, I think they had, I believe they had five salons here. You know where the money went. It went up before. Before I can remember.

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Karen told me that I should be sure to ask you about

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: the birth of your wife's first child.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the, is that the, do you have some story about that with the doctor?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Oh, well, there was a doctor.

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Myers. Well, money was scarce that day too. And I lived in the house. I worked for the, oh that's right, I put in four years on the dray too. I forgot about that. The dray service here, we had wood, coal and ice. Well, the doc came over and he sat there a couple of hours. I was getting kind of nervous.

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: I said, doc, ain't you gonna do something?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Well, nothing to do, just sit down and take it easy. Well, he stayed there until about midnight and then maybe arrived.

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: So, my girl, I said, doc, I said, would you want a cup of coffee or would you rather have a beer?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Oh, if you don't mind, I have a beer. I didn't have no beer. But lucky enough, I went down to, I think they had one place just frozen up so I got a beer for them. He liked beer. Well, I said, I guess it's about time to settle up, but I haven't got any money. Well, he said nobody has. But I said, I'll give you a thunder tool of coal and then maybe I can pay you a little and work the rest out. Well, it cost me, I figured, forty dollars. So, I thought that was much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that right during the depression?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: That was 1933.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you work on the dray during the depression? Is that what you did?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: I worked there from 1928 to 1932. I think it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How rough was it for people in town?

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Well, it was rough.

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: And then they got the Lester Rustics and the WPA and one thing and another going and they made a little money here. And they started putting in the sewer systems. The labors cost less than ten thousand dollars and it was all dug by hand. They were better with it. They couldn't dig across the streets here now that much.

speaker: Oscar Johnson
words: Listen, I don't want to keep you on the spot. Maybe there's some time when...

speaker: Oscar Johnson
